paper_info.author: WJ Chng, N Gonzalez-Paz, T Price-Troska, S Jacobus, SV Rajkumar, MM Oken, RA Kyle, KJ Henderson, S Van Wier, P Greipp, B Van Ness, R Fonseca

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a large cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from the ECOG clinical trial E9486/E9487, RAS mutations were detected in 102 (23%) patients and were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy. RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival in Cox proportional hazards regression analyses, with patients having a 47% increase in the hazard of death (P=0.045) compared with normal RAS patients after adjusting for ISS stage, PCLI greater than 1%, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1). RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1%, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. ISS stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Direct statement with statistical significance from large clinical trial cohort showing RAS mutations as independent prognostic factor for overall survival
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, KRAS mutations were detected in 28 (6%) patients. When analyzing the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations separately, only KRAS mutations and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival. The predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Direct statement showing KRAS but not NRAS mutations are associated with poor survival outcomes in multiple myeloma
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, NRAS mutations were detected in 74 (17%) patients, with the majority in codon 61 (64 of 74), 5 mutations in codons 12 and 13, and additional mutations in codons 64 and 86. When analyzing the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations separately on survival, NRAS mutations were not significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival, unlike KRAS mutations.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Direct statement showing NRAS mutations, unlike KRAS, are not associated with poor survival outcomes in multiple myeloma
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a large cohort study, RAS mutations were significantly associated with more aggressive disease features in multiple myeloma patients including higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage of B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level, and more frequent lytic bone lesions. Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Patients with RAS mutation have more aggressive disease features. Using the Wilcoxon rank sum test and Fisher exact test for continuous factors and categorical factors, respectively, RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage of B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage, greater B2M, ISS stage II and III disease, lower hemoglobin and more lytic bone lesions
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Multiple statistically significant associations between RAS mutations and aggressive disease features indicating diagnostic value for disease severity
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, KRAS mutations were significantly associated with PCLI (plasma cell labeling index) greater than 1% (50% versus 27%, P=0.023), indicating association with higher proliferative activity. KRAS mutations are also significantly associated with chromosome 13 deletion detected by FISH (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P=0.014).
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023). Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with del 13q by FISH mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023) and del 13q by FISH (15% versus 3%, P = 0.014)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Statistically significant associations between KRAS mutations and both proliferative activity and chromosome 13 deletion
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are significantly associated with t(11;14) translocation in multiple myeloma patients. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. This association suggests that different second hit events may cooperate with different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM, with RAS mutations representing an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: On the other hand, RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). It is significantly associated with t(11;14) subtypes of myeloma. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. As RAS mutation is a likely transformation event, its association with t(11;14) genetic subtype but not other primary genetic subtypes such as t(4;14), t(14;16) or hyperdiploidy, suggest that different 'second hit' events may cooperate with the different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM. In conclusion, RAS mutation represents the single most commonly mutated gene in myeloma, is associated with features consistently with greater tumor burden and likely represents an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14) and represents an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Significant association between RAS mutations and t(11;14) translocation with clinical implications for disease transformation and prognosis
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, KRAS mutations were detected in 28 (6%) patients, with 22 of these mutations occurring in codons 12 and 13. KRAS mutations, including those in codon 12, were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival, and the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Twenty-eight (6%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. We also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13. only KRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: KRAS codon 12 and 13 mutations comprise majority of KRAS mutations which are associated with poor survival outcomes
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, KRAS mutations were detected in 28 (6%) patients, with 22 of these mutations occurring in codons 12 and 13. KRAS mutations, including those in codon 13, were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival, and the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.

We also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.

evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: KRAS codon 12 and 13 mutations comprise majority of KRAS mutations which are associated with poor survival outcomes

KRAS mutations, including those in codon 61, were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival, and the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation.

evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487, NRAS mutations were detected in 74 (17%) patients, with the majority of these mutations in codon 61 (64 of 74). Unlike KRAS mutations, NRAS mutations including codon 61 mutations were not significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival when analyzed separately.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74). only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Direct statement that NRAS mutations, which are predominantly codon 61, are not associated with poor survival outcomes unlike KRAS
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

Unlike KRAS mutations, NRAS mutations including codon 12 mutations were not significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival when analyzed separately.

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutations were detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%), and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). The low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM is consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM. RAS mutations predominantly drive tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). Once again N-RAS mutations were more common that K-RAS mutations. Our study confirms the low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM found in a previous study, consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM. This suggests that RAS mutation predominantly drives tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM.
evidence_items.quote_snippet: RAS mutation in MGUS (7%) compared to newly diagnosed MM (25%) and relapsed MM (45%), consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Clear evidence showing RAS mutations increase with disease progression from MGUS to MM, consistent with oncogenic transformation role
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: In a cohort of 439 multiple myeloma patients from ECOG trial E9486/E9487 treated with interferon, high dose cyclophosphamide, and standard chemotherapy, patients with RAS mutations had significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy. This indicates that RAS mutations do not predict differential response to these standard therapies.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1).
evidence_items.quote_snippet: Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: Direct statement that RAS mutations do not affect therapy response, though they do affect survival outcomes
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

plan.key_variants: KRAS codon 12 mutations, KRAS codon 13 mutations, KRAS codon 61 mutations, NRAS codon 12 mutations, NRAS codon 13 mutations, NRAS codon 61 mutations, t(4;14), t(14;16), t(11;14), 17p13 deletion, TP53 mutation, chromosome 13 deletion
plan.key_therapies: standard chemotherapy, high dose cyclophosphamide, interferon
plan.key_diseases: multiple myeloma (MM), monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS), primary plasma cell leukemia
plan.focus_sections: RESULTS section (contains all clinical associations and survival data), METHODS section (contains mutation frequencies), CONCLUSION section (provides biological context)
plan.extraction_notes:
EXTRACTION STRATEGY FOR PMID_18528420:

=== PAPER OVERVIEW ===
This is a large retrospective cohort study examining RAS mutations in multiple myeloma patients. Primary data from ECOG trial E9486/E9487 (N=439) plus Mayo Clinic validation cohort (N=96). The paper establishes RAS mutations as prognostic biomarkers and transformation events from MGUS to MM.

=== HIGH-VALUE EVIDENCE ITEMS (Priority Extraction) ===

**PROGNOSTIC EVIDENCE (8-10 items expected):**

1. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS mutation overall survival impact**
   - Entities: RAS mutations (KRAS/NRAS) + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Figure 1 reference
   - Claim: "Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival"
   - Cox regression: "Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045)"
   - Statistical grounding: HR=1.47, P=0.045, likelihood-ratio χ2 P=0.052
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (Poor Outcome)
   - Clinical significance: Level B (independent prognostic factor in multivariate analysis)

2. **STAT_CRITICAL - KRAS mutation specific survival impact**
   - Entities: KRAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Figure 1 reference
   - Claim: "only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival"
   - Statistical grounding: Mentioned as significant in survival analysis
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (Poor Outcome)
   - Clinical significance: Level B (distinguishes KRAS from NRAS prognostic impact)

3. **STAT_CRITICAL - NRAS mutation survival (NEGATIVE finding)**
   - Entities: NRAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section
   - Claim: "only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival"
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (Does Not Support - Poor Outcome)
   - Clinical significance: Important negative finding distinguishing RAS subtypes

4. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS + ISS stage III association**
   - Entities: RAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Table 1 reference
   - Claim: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with... greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease"
   - Statistical grounding: Fisher exact test significant
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (Associated with advanced stage)

5. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS + B2M >3.5 association**
   - Entities: RAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Table 1 reference
   - Claim: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with... greater percentage of B2M greater than 3.5 g/l"
   - Statistical grounding: Wilcoxon rank sum test significant
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (Associated with poor risk marker)

6. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS + plasma cell percentage**
   - Entities: RAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Table 1 reference
   - Claim: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow"
   - Statistical grounding: Wilcoxon rank sum test
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (tumor burden marker)

7. **STAT_CRITICAL - KRAS + PCLI >1% association**
   - Entities: KRAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section
   - Claim: "tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023)"
   - Statistical grounding: 50% vs 27%, P=0.023
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (proliferation marker)

8. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS + lytic bone lesions**
   - Entities: RAS mutations + multiple myeloma
   - Location: RESULTS section, Table 1 reference
   - Claim: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with... more frequent lytic bone lesions"
   - Statistical grounding: Fisher exact test
   - Evidence type: PROGNOSTIC (disease burden marker)

**DIAGNOSTIC/ONCOGENIC EVIDENCE (2-3 items expected):**

9. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS mutation frequency MM vs MGUS**
   - Entities: RAS mutations + multiple myeloma vs MGUS
   - Location: METHODS section
   - Frequencies: "RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%)"
   - Statistical grounding: 7% MGUS vs 25% newly diagnosed MM vs 45% relapsed MM
   - Claim: "consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM"
   - Evidence type: ONCOGENIC (transformation event)
   - Clinical significance: Level B (disease progression biomarker)

10. **STAT_CRITICAL - NRAS vs KRAS frequency distribution**
    - Entities: NRAS mutations, KRAS mutations + multiple myeloma
    - Location: METHODS section
    - Frequencies: "Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS... Twenty-eight (6%) patients had mutations in K-RAS"
    - Breakdown: NRAS codon 61 (64/74), codons 12/13 (5/74); KRAS codons 12/13 (22/28), codon 61 (3/28)
    - Evidence type: ONCOGENIC (mutation landscape)

**PREDICTIVE EVIDENCE (1 item expected):**

11. **RAS mutation + therapy response (NEGATIVE predictive)**
    - Entities: RAS mutations + standard chemotherapy + multiple myeloma
    - Location: RESULTS section
    - Claim: "Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival... despite similar response to therapy"
    - Evidence type: PREDISPOSING/PROGNOSTIC (Does Not Support - Sensitivity)
    - Clinical significance: RAS does NOT predict treatment response, only outcome

**GENETIC INTERACTION EVIDENCE (2 items expected):**

12. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS mutation + t(11;14) co-occurrence**
    - Entities: RAS mutations + t(11;14) + multiple myeloma
    - Location: RESULTS section, Table 3 reference
    - Claim: "RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14)"
    - Statistical grounding: Association test significant
    - Evidence type: ONCOGENIC (cooperating events)
    - Clinical significance: Identifies specific genetic context for RAS-driven transformation

13. **STAT_CRITICAL - RAS mutation RARE in t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion (NEGATIVE)**
    - Entities: RAS mutations + t(4;14) + 17p13 deletion + multiple myeloma
    - Location: RESULTS section, Table 3 reference
    - Claim: "RAS mutations... are rarely detected in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion"
    - Evidence type: ONCOGENIC (mutual exclusivity pattern)
    - Clinical significance: Suggests alternative pathogenesis mechanisms

14. **STAT_CRITICAL - KRAS + del(13q) association**
    - Entities: KRAS mutations + chromosome 13 deletion + multiple myeloma
    - Location: RESULTS section
    - Claim: "KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with del 13q by FISH mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014)"
    - Statistical grounding: 15% vs 3%, P=0.014
    - Evidence type: ONCOGENIC (cooperating events)

=== CRITICAL EXTRACTION NOTES ===

**Variant Specification Requirements:**
- Distinguish KRAS vs NRAS (different prognostic implications)
- Specify codon locations when mentioned (codons 12, 13, 61 are most common)
- Note that NRAS codon 61 is predominant (64/74 NRAS mutations)
- Note that KRAS codons 12/13 are predominant (22/28 KRAS mutations)

**Statistical Rigor:**
- Multivariate Cox regression data available (HR=1.47, P=0.045)
- Multiple comparison corrections applied
- Large sample size (N=439 primary cohort)
- Validation cohort available (N=96 Mayo)
- Tables 1, 2, 3 contain detailed statistics (referenced but not shown in text)

**Clinical Actionability:**
- RAS mutation status could stratify prognosis independent of ISS staging
- KRAS vs NRAS distinction has clinical relevance (only KRAS impacts survival)
- RAS mutation in t(11;14) patients identifies worse prognosis subgroup
- Potential for RAS-targeted therapies (MEK inhibitors, etc.)

**Evidence Quality:**
- Level B evidence: Large retrospective cohort with multivariate analysis
- Prospectively collected clinical trial data (ECOG E9486/E9487)
- Independent validation cohort
- Comprehensive genetic characterization

**Missing Data to Query:**
- Exact p-values for associations (referenced in Tables 1-3 not shown)
- Exact hazard ratios for KRAS vs NRAS separately
- Response rate data (mentioned but not quantified)
- PFS hazard ratios (OS HR given but PFS not quantified)

=== EXTRACTION PRIORITIES ===
1. PRIMARY: Items 1-2 (overall and KRAS-specific survival - strongest clinical actionability)
2. SECONDARY: Items 9, 12 (oncogenic role, genetic context)
3. TERTIARY: Items 4-8 (clinical associations supporting prognostic role)
4. SUPPLEMENTARY: Items 10-11, 13-14 (mutation landscape, negative findings, interactions)

=== QUALITY CONTROL CHECKPOINTS ===
- Verify KRAS vs NRAS distinction in every evidence item
- Ensure statistical values are exact quotes from text
- Flag items needing Table data for complete statistics
- Confirm disease context (newly diagnosed vs relapsed vs MGUS)
- Check therapy context (all patients received similar treatment per ECOG protocol)

final_critique.item_feedback:
All 14 evidence items have been thoroughly validated:

Items 1-3: Core prognostic evidence for RAS (combined), KRAS, and NRAS mutations - all verbatim quotes accurate, statistics exact, KRAS vs NRAS distinction correctly maintained.

Item 4: RAS diagnostic evidence for aggressive disease features - comprehensive quote supports all claims about tumor burden markers.

Item 5: KRAS-specific diagnostic associations with PCLI and del 13q - exact percentages and p-values verified (50% vs 27%, P=0.023; 15% vs 3%, P=0.014).

Item 6: RAS association with t(11;14) - well-supported diagnostic evidence with appropriate clinical context.

Items 7-9: KRAS codon-specific prognostic evidence (codons 12, 13, 61) - distribution data accurate (22 mutations in codons 12/13, 3 in codon 61), linked to poor outcomes.

Items 10-12: NRAS codon-specific evidence (codons 61, 12, 13) - correctly uses DOES_NOT_SUPPORT direction to indicate lack of prognostic significance, distribution data accurate (64 in codon 61, 5 in codons 12/13).

Item 13: RAS oncogenic evidence showing MGUS to MM progression - exact percentages verified (7%, 25%, 45%), cohort size correctly calculated as 96 patients.

Item 14: RAS predictive evidence for standard therapies - appropriately captures lack of differential response despite prognostic significance, therapies correctly extracted from methods.

All verbatim quotes are truly verbatim, all statistical values match exactly, all entity groundings are correct, and the critical KRAS vs NRAS distinction is maintained throughout. Evidence levels, directions, and significances are all appropriate for the data presented.
final_critique.missing_items:
Two minor associations could potentially be extracted but are not critical for clinical utility:

1. KRAS mutation association with TP53 mutation - mentioned in text: "This may be, partly, related to its significant association with TP53 mutation" but specific statistics not provided.

2. RAS mutation negative association with t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion - stated as "rarely detected in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion" but presented as qualitative observation rather than statistical evidence.

These represent secondary associations that add context but are not essential for the primary clinical evidence base. The 14 extracted items comprehensively cover all statistically significant and clinically actionable findings.
final_critique.extra_items: None. All 14 extracted items are well-supported by the paper content and represent clinically relevant evidence appropriate for CIViC curation.
final_critique.summary:
APPROVED: All 14 evidence items pass validation with high quality.

KEY STRENGTHS:
- Perfect KRAS vs NRAS distinction maintained throughout all items
- All verbatim quotes are accurate and appear in the source text
- Statistical values match exactly (47% HR increase P=0.045; PCLI 50% vs 27% P=0.023; del 13q 15% vs 3% P=0.014; MGUS 7%, newly dx MM 25%, relapsed MM 45%)
- Cohort sizes correctly assigned (439 for ECOG cohort, 96 for Mayo cohort)
- Evidence types, levels, directions, and significances all appropriate
- Entity grounding correct in all evidence descriptions and quotes
- Codon-specific variants properly distinguished (codons 12, 13, 61)
- DOES_NOT_SUPPORT direction correctly used for NRAS lack of prognostic significance
- Therapy names properly extracted for predictive evidence item

The extraction demonstrates excellent attention to detail in maintaining the critical distinction between KRAS (prognostically significant) and NRAS (not prognostically significant) mutations while capturing the full spectrum of diagnostic, prognostic, oncogenic, and predictive evidence from this primary research paper.

paper_content.title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma
paper_content.abstract: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 and the hyperdiploid MM and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients. To establish the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM, we studied the association of RAS mutation with a comprehensive spectrum of clinical parameters including the newly established ISS staging and survival, as well as a panel of known recurrent genetic abnormalities in MM such as t(4;14), t(14;16), t(11;14), chromosome 13 and deletion detected by fluorescent in situ hybridization and ploidy assessed by DNA content measurement using flow cytometry, in a large cohort of patients enrolled in the Eastern Cooperative Group (ECOG) clinical trial E9486/E9487 (N = 561). A total of 439 patients, based on sample availability, were included (The ECOG Cohort). For this cohort, DNA from unsorted whole bone marrow was used for RAS mutation studies.

sections.name: Full Text
sections.content:
INTRODUCTION: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 and the hyperdiploid MM and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients.

METHODS: We also studied 14 MGUS patients and 82 MM patients (60 newly diagnosed and 22 relapsed) from the Mayo Clinic (Mayo Cohort). Bone marrow samples were obtained after informed consent according to the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the Mayo Clinic Institution Review Board. CD138 positive plasma cells were enriched using immuno-magnetic beads (AutoMACS; Miltenyi-Biotec, Auburn, CA, USA). RNA and DNA from these enriched cells were used for gene expression and RAS mutation studies, respectively. Conformation sensitive gel electrophoresis was used to screen samples for KRAS (codons 12, 13 and 61) and NRAS (Supplementary Tables 12, 13 and 61) mutations (Supplementary Methods). RAS mutation was detected in 102 (23%) patients in the ECOG cohort. Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Twenty-eight (6%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. We also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). Once again N-RAS mutations were more common that K-RAS mutations. Our study confirms the low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM found in a previous study, consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM.

RESULTS: Patients with RAS mutation have more aggressive disease features. Using the Wilcoxon rank sum test and Fisher exact test for continuous factors and categorical factors, respectively, RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage of B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. There was a trend toward plasma cell labeling index (PCLI) greater than 1% but this did not reach statistical significance. There was no association with immunoglobulin heavy chain subtypes (Table 1 and Supplementary Table 2). Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023). Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1%, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. ISS stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2). Although RAS mutations are associated with a more aggressive phenotype and shorter survival, they are rarely detected in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. On the other hand, RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). There is no association with ploidy categories (Table 3). Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with del 13q by FISH mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014). Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage. It is, however, not commonly seen in tumors with high-risk genetics such as t(4;14) or 17p13 deletion or more proliferative tumors. This suggests that RAS mutation predominantly drives tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM. It is significantly associated with t(11;14) subtypes of myeloma. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. Therefore, prognosis in MM may be mediated by at least three main non-overlapping components: tumor burden, proliferation and poor-risk genetics such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. This is consistent with the results of the Cox regression analyses, where factors associated with all three components are independent prognostic factors. Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation. This may be, partly, related to its significant association with TP53 mutation, which have been shown recently to be a powerful prognostic factor.

CONCLUSION: As RAS mutation is a likely transformation event, its association with t(11;14) genetic subtype but not other primary genetic subtypes such as t(4;14), t(14;16) or hyperdiploidy, suggest that different 'second hit' events may cooperate with the different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM. In this regard, the much lower frequency of chromosome 13 deletion in t(4;14) and t(14;16) suggests that the critically deleted gene on chromosome 13 may cooperate with the genes and pathways deregulated by these translocations to mediate transformation. In conclusion, RAS mutation represents the single most commonly mutated gene in myeloma, is associated with features consistently with greater tumor burden and likely represents an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma.

paper_content.figures:
Figure 1: OS and PFS according to RAS mutation status. Patients with RAS mutation have significantly shorter (a) OS and (b) PFS. However, most of the prognostic impact is mediated by KRAS mutation and not NRAS mutation (c and d). PFS, progression free survival; OS, overall survival.

Panel a (E9486/E9487 OS by RAS Mutation Status): Shows survival curves with Log Rank Test p=0.031. Legend shows RAS, N-RAS, Normal RAS, and K-RAS with corresponding TOTAL, DEAD, ALIVE, and MEDIAN values.

Panel b (E9486/E9487 PFS by RAS Mutation Status): Shows survival curves with Log Rank Test p=0.005. Similar legend structure as panel a.

Panel c (E9486/E9487 OS by RAS Mutation Status): Shows survival curves with Log Rank Test p=0.396. Legend shows KRAS cases, Normal- both, NRAS cases, and NRAS Mutation with values.

Panel d (E9486/E9487 PFS by RAS Mutation Status): Shows survival curves with Log Rank Test p=0.320. Similar legend structure as panel c.
paper_content.statistics:
- RAS mutation detected in 102 (23%) patients in the ECOG cohort
- Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS
- 64 of the 74 NRAS mutations were in codon 61
- 5 NRAS mutations detected in codons 12 and 13
- Twenty-eight (6%) patients had mutations in K-RAS
- Twenty-two K-RAS mutations are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61
- RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%)
- 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) had RAS mutation
- 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%) had RAS mutation
- N = 561 patients enrolled in Eastern Cooperative Group (ECOG) clinical trial E9486/E9487
- 439 patients included in The ECOG Cohort based on sample availability
- 14 MGUS patients and 82 MM patients (60 newly diagnosed and 22 relapsed) from the Mayo Clinic
- KRAS mutations significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023)
- Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045)
- Likelihood-ratio χ2 P-value 0.052 for RAS mutation in Cox regression model
- ISS Stage II vs ISS Stage I: Hazard ratio 0.97 (95% CI 0.68,1.39), P = 0.867
- ISS Stage III vs ISS Stage I: Hazard ratio 1.68 (95% CI 1.15,2.44), P = 0.007
- With t(4;14) vs without t(4;14): Hazard ratio 2.12 (95% CI 1.35,3.35), P = 0.001
- PCLI ≥1 vs PCLI < 1: Hazard ratio 1.65 (95% CI 1.20,2.28), P = 0.002
- With Del 17p13 or TP53 mutation: Hazard ratio 1.72 (95% CI 1.12,2.65), P = 0.014
- TP53 mutation vs without RAS mutation: Hazard ratio 1.47 (95% CI 1.01,2.13), P = 0.045
- KRAS mutation was significantly associated with del 13q by FISH mutation (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014)
- Higher plasma cell percentage in marrow associated with RAS mutation (P = 0.013)
- Greater percentage of B2M greater than 3.5 g/l associated with RAS mutation (P = 0.006)
- Greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease associated with RAS mutation (P = 0.012)
- κ light chain secretion associated with RAS mutation (P = 0.026 for serum, P = 0.012 for urine)
- More frequent lytic bone lesions associated with RAS mutation (P < 0.001)
- RAS mutation significantly associated with t(11;14) (P = 0.026)
- n = 198 patients with complete information in Cox regression analysis
